name: Dashavatar
description: Dashavatar refers to the ten primary incarnations (avatars) of Vishnu, the Hindu god of preservation. Vishnu descends to Earth in these forms to restore cosmic order and righteousness (Dharma) whenever it is threatened by evil forces. These avatars represent different stages of evolution, from aquatic life to human forms, demonstrating Vishnu's presence and intervention in various epochs. The ten avatars are: Matsya (fish), Kurma (tortoise), Varaha (boar), Narasimha (half-man/half-lion), Vamana (dwarf), Parashurama (warrior with an axe), Rama (righteous king), Krishna (divine statesman), Buddha (enlightened teacher), and Kalki (the future avatar). Each avatar has a specific purpose, usually to defeat a powerful demon or restore balance to the world. The concept of Dashavatar highlights the cyclical nature of time and the continuous struggle between good and evil, with Vishnu intervening to ensure the triumph of Dharma. These avatars are revered in various Hindu traditions and their stories are central to Hindu scriptures and folklore. The Dashavataras are a powerful symbol of hope and divine intervention in the face of adversity, embodying Vishnu's compassion and commitment to upholding cosmic order.
subtitle: Ten Incarnations of Vishnu

keyMyths.title: Matsya Avatar and the Great Flood
keyMyths.description: Vishnu takes the form of a fish (Matsya) to warn Manu about an impending great flood and instructs him to build a boat to save humanity and the seeds of all living beings. This avatar ensures the continuation of life after the deluge.

keyMyths.title: Narasimha Avatar and the Defeat of Hiranyakashipu
keyMyths.description: To protect his devotee Prahlada, Vishnu manifests as Narasimha, a half-man/half-lion, to kill the demon king Hiranyakashipu, who had been granted a boon that made him invulnerable to almost all beings and weapons. Narasimha kills him at twilight, neither inside nor outside, neither on land nor in the air, fulfilling the conditions of the boon.

keyMyths.title: Rama Avatar and the Triumph over Ravana
keyMyths.description: Vishnu incarnates as Rama, a righteous prince, to defeat the demon king Ravana, who had abducted Rama's wife, Sita. Rama's story is a testament to Dharma, courage, and the ultimate victory of good over evil, as depicted in the epic Ramayana.
